title: International Datathon on Animal Health Organised by the World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH)
text: Welcome to the WOAH Datathon web page!

title: International Datathon on Animal Health Organised by the World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH)
section: Overview
text:
Overview
The World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH) invites multidisciplinary teams to participate in an international datathon on animal health data science, focusing on surveillance, risk, trade and impact assessment.
Participants will work remotely over several months to address real‑world analytical challenges using pilot datasets provided by WOAH and partners. The datathon aims to stimulate innovation, methodological rigor and decision‑relevant analytics in animal health.
Winning teams will receive prize money, the opportunity to be featured in the Animal Echo and to present their work at a dedicated side event during the WOAH Global Session in 2027.

title: International Datathon on Animal Health Organised by the World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH)
section: Who Can Participate
text:
Who Can Participate
The datathon is open to international, multidisciplinary teams (1–6 members), including but not limited to:

Data scientists and statisticians
Epidemiologists
Economists
Computer scientists
Public health and animal health professionals

Teams may include members from academia (students or faculty), public institutions, international organisations, NGOs or the private sector.

title: International Datathon on Animal Health Organised by the World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH)
section: Datathon Format
text:
Datathon Format

Launch: Q2 2026
Duration: 3–4 months (remote participation)
Team work: Independent and asynchronous
Support: Documentation, data descriptions and periodic technical clarifications

Final submission: Analytical pipeline + results report on a git repo by September 30, 2026

title: International Datathon on Animal Health Organised by the World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH)
section: Data & Outputs
text:
Data & Outputs
Each team will:

Select one challenge either from the list or self-proposed (subject to approval)
Develop a complete data science pipeline, including data processing, modelling and validation
Produce analytical outputs based on pilot or historical datasets
Document methods and results in a concise technical report
All code must be reproducible and submitted in a public or shared repository.

Contact: dataintegration.dept @ woah.org

title: Datathon Deliverable Assessment Criteria
text: The material submitted submitted by each team will be assessed by a panel of experts based on the following criteria:

title: Datathon Deliverable Assessment Criteria
section: Scientific/Technical Accuracy
text:
Scientific/Technical Accuracy
Definition: Measures correctness and rigor of the methods and outputs.
Key Points:

Appropriateness of modelling approach for the problem.
Correct handling of data (e.g., preprocessing, imputation, bias correction).
Logical and reproducible workflow from raw data to final output.
Validation against known or simulated benchmarks.

Scoring Tip: 0–5 scale; 5 = methodologically robust, fully validated, no obvious flaws.

title: Datathon Deliverable Assessment Criteria
section: Innovation and Creativity
text:
Innovation and Creativity
Definition: Measures originality in problem-solving, methodology, or data integration.
Key Points:

Novel application of statistical, ML, or network techniques.
Creative integration of multiple data sources.
Unique visualization or decision-support approach.
Evidence of thinking beyond “textbook” solutions.

Scoring Tip: 0–5 scale; 5 = clearly innovative, methodologically or conceptually novel.

title: Datathon Deliverable Assessment Criteria
section: Practical Relevance / Policy Impact
text:
Practical Relevance / Policy Impact
Definition: Measures the degree to which outputs address WOAH priorities or real-world decision-making needs.
Key Points:

Alignment with animal health policy, surveillance, or trade risk objectives.
Interpretability for non-technical stakeholders.
Clarity of actionable insights or recommendations.
Responsiveness to intended problem scope.

Scoring Tip: 0–5 scale; 5 = deliverables are directly actionable and relevant.

title: Datathon Deliverable Assessment Criteria
section: Reproducibility and Documentation
text:
Reproducibility and Documentation
Definition: Measures clarity and completeness of the workflow, code, and reporting.
Key Points:

Availability of scripts/notebooks and clear instructions.
Documentation of data sources, assumptions, and parameters.
Ability for independent evaluators to reproduce results.
Versioning or modular design supporting reuse.

Scoring Tip: 0–5 scale; 5 = fully reproducible and well-documented workflow.

title: List of datathon challenges
text:
Objective:
Strengthen the capacity of WOAH and its Members to anticipate, detect and respond to emerging animal health threats by integrating early detection, nowcasting and short-term forecasting approaches. This challenge addresses issues such as delayed reporting, incomplete data and poor early warnings, which restrict situational awareness during the initial onset of outbreaks. Timely and reliable epidemic intelligence is essential for transparency, risk management and maintaining trust among members, trading partners and the international community. Improved real-time insight enables proportionate response measures and informed decision-making, thereby reinforcing WOAH’s role as a global reference for animal health information.
Data:
Data are restricted to a few priority diseases (e.g. Avian influenza, African swine fever) available from

WAHIS historical event notifications
Syndromic surveillance indicators
Trade, movement (FAOSTAT, UN Comtrade), environmental (ERA5, WorldClim), and contextual proxies (FAOSTAT, WorldBank, FAO HiH Geospatial data (land cover, crop & livestock-related layers, Gridded Livestock of the World (GLW)
Global Forest Watch data portal – deforestation, active fire, land change indicators (API & downloads): https://www.globalforestwatch.org/ )
SPS notifications
Simulation exercises (Simex) for validation / comparison of the pipeline developed
Media or digital signal proxies (EIOS)

Deliverable:
An integrated analytical workflow that produces early warning signals for specific diseases and provides corrected, real-time estimates of disease dynamics and short-term forecasts of disease evolution, with the level of uncertainty being explicitly quantified.

title: List of datathon challenges
section: 2 AI-assisted signal extraction from event-based surveillance text, media reports, etc.
text:
2 AI-assisted signal extraction from event-based surveillance text, media reports, etc.
Objective:
Enhance epidemic intelligence by using artificial intelligence to extract relevant information on animal health from unstructured text sources. This challenge addresses the need to make better use of the rapidly growing informal and semi-structured information streams that can complement official surveillance systems. Strengthening event-based surveillance enables earlier identification of potential threats while respecting and integrating officially validated information. This challenge contributes to more agile horizon scanning and improved global preparedness.
Data:

News articles and media reports (GDELT ,RSS feeds from veterinary journals, EIOS, Social media)
Event-based surveillance feeds

Deliverable:
A system that can identify, classify and summarise animal health events from text sources.

title: List of datathon challenges
section: 3 Adjusting WOAH Monitoring Indicators using input-level indices to reduce structural bias
text:
3 Adjusting WOAH Monitoring Indicators using input-level indices to reduce structural bias
Objective:
Create a denominator or input‑level index representing the capacities and efforts of national Veterinary Services so that existing output indicators – disease reporting and other sanitary measures to control animal diseases or to improve animal welfare, which are considered as implementation of WOAH standards – can be normalised and compared more fairly across WOAH Membership. Scientifically, this addresses heterogeneity in data generation processes. Politically, fair and transparent comparisons are essential to avoid misinterpretation of indicators, protect trust among Members, and ensure that monitoring outputs are used constructively to support improvement rather than placing barriers to trade.
Data:

WAHIS disease situation and control measure data
Observatory indicators and monitoring outputs
Number of veterinary professionals extracted from IPUMS
WAHIS and FAOSTAT animal population data
World Bank Agricultural GDP and total GDP
World Bank income level

Deliverable:
A methodology or tool that distinguishes genuine differences in surveillance/implementation from artefacts caused by unequal human, financial, or system resources.
.

title: List of datathon challenges
section: 4 Trade Vulnerability and Disease Risk Along Trade and Movement Networks
text:
4 Trade Vulnerability and Disease Risk Along Trade and Movement Networks
Objective:
Assess how animal health events interact with trade and movement networks to generate vulnerability and disruption. Scientifically, this integrates network analysis with disease risk assessment. Politically, it informs discussions on trade resilience, proportional sanitary measures, and risk-based decision-making consistent with international standards.
Data:

International and regional trade data
Animal movement proxies and network data
WAHIS surveillance and outbreak data
Livestock population, density, and production data (FAOSTAT, Gridded Livestock of the World (GLW))
Geographic and administrative boundary data (WOAH internal)
WTO (SPS notifications)
Air, maritime transport (OpenSky,AIS)
Road density (Open street map, WB infrastructure data (?))
Regional (Mercosur, asean, eurostat)

Deliverable:
Indicators, analyses, or dashboards identifying vulnerable trade links and pathways associated with animal health shocks.

title: List of datathon challenges
section: 5 Understanding misinformation and disinformation in animal health.
text:
5 Understanding misinformation and disinformation in animal health.
Objective:
Improve understanding of the nature, drivers and dynamics of mis- and disinformation in the field of animal health in order to safeguard the effectiveness of disease prevention and control measures. From a scientific perspective, this challenge acknowledges the increasing awareness that information dynamics influence epidemiological outcomes by shaping perceptions, behaviours, and compliance with control measures. Mis- and disinformation can undermine trust in official institutions, weaken adherence to international standards and complicate crisis management during outbreaks. Generating evidence on how false or misleading narratives emerge and spread will support WOAH and its Members to communicate more proactively, transparently, and credibly.
Data:

Official outbreak and disease information from WAHIS
World Organisation for Animal Health press releases
Food and Agriculture Organization outbreak updates
World Health Organization zoonotic communications (DON)
Media news articles and other publicly available information sources ( GDELT
Media Cloud
RSS aggregators
Social Media
National news archives
Time-stamped contextual data related to major outbreak events

Deliverable:
Analytical outputs that (i) identify and classify prevalent types of mis- and disinformation in animal health and associated diseases, (ii) describe how such narratives evolve over time, particularly during high-impact outbreaks, and (iii) propose indicators or scoring frameworks to detect early signals, high-risk topics, sources, geographical areas, and diseases where mis- and disinformation is more likely to occur.

title: List of datathon challenges
section: 6 Trends in animal disease occurrence
text:
6 Trends in animal disease occurrence
Objectives
Identify trends and variations in trends in animal disease occurrence when looking at the entire data set since 2005 up to late 2024. What is normal, what are expected or significant variations. Can be done globally or regionally. For some of the important diseases.
Is there any statistical association between disease events and meteorological/climatological events (Worldclim data)?
By having this, we could identify an early warning sign that could alert countries pre-emptively.
Data
WAHIS data
Deliverables
A retrospective statistical analysis of WAHIS data from 2005.

title: List of datathon challenges
section: 7 Forecasting Antimicrobials Intended for Use in Animals
text:
7 Forecasting Antimicrobials Intended for Use in Animals
Objective Explore the global patterns and trends in antimicrobial use (AMU) intended for use in animals using the ANIMUSE Global Database. AMU data (expressed in mg of antimicrobials adjusted by kg of animal biomass – mg/kg) will be used to identify spatial and temporal patterns across WOAH Members that have decided to be public and explore the distribution of antimicrobial classes used in food-producing animals. Country differences on antimicrobial classes, long-term trends and potential clusters of high or low AMU levels will be analysed.
Data

Antimicrobial use data reported by WOAH Members through the ANIMUSE System (both at Global level and for those Members decided to be public)
Antimicrobial quantities (kg)
Reporting coverage and adjusted antimicrobial quantities
Animal biomass denominator
Antimicrobial use adjusted by animal biomass (mg/kg)
Distribution of antimicrobial classes.

Deliverables
Participants will analyse the dataset and produce short analytical outputs addressing key questions on antimicrobial use patterns. Deliverables may include:

analysis of global and regional trends in AMU intensity (mg/kg)
cross-country comparisons of antimicrobial consumption
assessment of the relative contribution of antimicrobial classes to total AMU
identification of Members with high or low AMU intensity
Forecasting mg/kg by Members and WOAH regions by 2050
brief interpretation of observed trends and methodological considerations when analysing national AMU data.

title: List of datathon challenges
section: 8 AI-Assisted Extraction and Analysis of Global Veterinary Antibiotics
text:
8 AI-Assisted Extraction and Analysis of Global Veterinary Antibiotics
Objective Strengthen antimicrobial surveillance and support data verification by using artificial intelligence to extract, standardise, and consolidate information on veterinary antibiotic products from diverse, publicly available sources. The ultimate goal is to build a reliable global repository that can be used as a reference to verify national antimicrobial use (AMU) data submission.
Data

Official national antibiotic registration websites
Regional veterinary product databases
Pharmaceutical company product information and package inserts
Other reputable online sources with validated product information

Deliverables: An AI-powered system capable of automatically extracting product name, package size, active ingredient, concentration, route of administration, and target animal species and consolidating these into a structured global repository. Analytical output will highlight regional and global trends, identifying the most widely used products. For example, if a small subset of products accounts for the majority of veterinary antibiotics across countries, these can serve as priority references for data verification, reducing the need for complete global coverage of the repository.

title: List of datathon challenges
text: Below is a list of challenges that are provided for guidance or source of inspiration. Once a topic is chosen by a team, the outline does not have to be followed strictly, excursions in some of the other topics and beyond are welcome.

title: List of datathon challenges
section: 1 Early Outbreak Detection from Multi-Source Surveillance Data
text:
1 Early Outbreak Detection from Multi-Source Surveillance Data
Objective: Develop models for early detection of emerging animal health events.
Data:

WAHIS event notifications (historical)
Syndromic surveillance indicators
Trade/movement proxies
Environmental covariates

Methods:

Bayesian change-point detection
Spatio-temporal models
Anomaly detection

LSTM / transformer-based time series models

Deliverable:
End-to-end pipeline producing alerts with uncertainty quantification and interpretability.

title: List of datathon challenges
section: 2 Spatio-Temporal Risk Mapping of Priority Transboundary Animal Diseases
text:
2 Spatio-Temporal Risk Mapping of Priority Transboundary Animal Diseases
Objective:

Produce dynamic risk maps for a selected priority disease (e.g. ASF, HPAI, FMD).
Data:


Historical outbreak locations

Livestock density

Climate data

Trade and movement indicators

Methods:


Hierarchical Bayesian models

Gaussian processes

Spatial ML (e.g. graph neural networks)

Deliverable:

Reproducible pipeline generating predictive risk surfaces and validation metrics.

title: List of datathon challenges
section: 3 Nowcasting and Forecasting Disease Incidence Under Reporting Delays
text:
3 Nowcasting and Forecasting Disease Incidence Under Reporting Delays
Objective:

Correct for under-reporting and reporting delays in animal disease data.
Data:


Time-stamped notification data with known lags

Partial reporting histories

Methods:


Bayesian nowcasting

State-space models

Probabilistic forecasting

Deliverable:

Forecasts with credible intervals and comparison of methodological approaches.

title: List of datathon challenges
section: 4 AI-Assisted Signal Extraction from Event-Based Surveillance Text
text:
4 AI-Assisted Signal Extraction from Event-Based Surveillance Text
Objective:

Extract and classify animal health signals from unstructured text sources.
Data:


News articles

Official reports

Social media or RSS feeds (curated sample)

Methods:


NLP pipelines

Transformer-based classification

Named entity recognition

Weak supervision

Deliverable:

Automated system producing structured signals linked to diseases, species, and locations.

title: List of datathon challenges
section: 5 Cross-Country Comparability and Bias Assessment in Surveillance Data
text:
5 Cross-Country Comparability and Bias Assessment in Surveillance Data

Quantify and adjust for heterogeneity in surveillance capacity and reporting practices.
Data:

Methods:


Latent variable models

Causal inference

Domain adaptation techniques

Deliverable:

Metrics and visualizations highlighting comparability issues and adjusted estimates.

title: List of datathon challenges
section: 6 Antimicrobial Use and Resistance: Pattern Discovery and Prediction
text:
6 Antimicrobial Use and Resistance: Pattern Discovery and Prediction
Objective:

Explore relationships between antimicrobial use (AMU) and resistance (AMR) indicators.
Data:

Methods:

Causal graphs

Deliverable:

Analytical pipeline identifying patterns, risk factors, and predictive signals.

title: List of datathon challenges
section: 7 One Health Integration: Linking Animal Health, Environment, and Climate Signals
text:
7 One Health Integration: Linking Animal Health, Environment, and Climate Signals
Objective:

Assess how environmental and climate variables modulate animal disease risk.
Data:

Deliverable:

title: List of datathon challenges
section: 8 Automated Quality Assessment of Animal Health Data Submissions
text:
8 Automated Quality Assessment of Animal Health Data Submissions

Data:


Historical surveillance submissions with known data quality issues

title: List of datathon challenges

text:
9 Scenario Modelling for Disease Spread Under Control Strategies

title: List of datathon challenges
section: 15 Harmonising Epidemiological and Economic Metrics Across Countries
text:
15 Harmonising Epidemiological and Economic Metrics Across Countries
Challenge:

Build a framework linking epidemiological indicators (incidence, mortality) to economic burden metrics.
Data:


Multi-country pilot datasets

Methods:


Metric harmonisation

Scaling and standardisation

title: List of datathon challenges
section: 16 Trade Disruptions Following Animal Health Events
text:
16 Trade Disruptions Following Animal Health Events
Problem:

Animal disease outbreaks often lead to asymmetric and poorly quantified trade impacts.
Objective:

Quantify how outbreaks affect bilateral and regional trade flows over time.
Data (pilot):


Disease outbreak timelines

Aggregated livestock/product trade data

Country and product metadata

Methods:


Interrupted time series

Difference-in-differences

Bayesian causal models

Deliverables:


Quantified trade impact estimates

Visual timelines of disruption and recovery

Methodological note on attribution

title: List of datathon challenges
section: 17 Predicting Trade Vulnerability to Animal Health Shocks
text:
17 Predicting Trade Vulnerability to Animal Health Shocks
Problem:

Some trade relationships are more fragile than others, but vulnerability is rarely quantified.
Objective:

Develop indicators of trade vulnerability to animal health events.
Data (pilot):


Trade networks

Historical outbreaks

Production and dependency proxies

Methods:


Network analysis

Risk scoring models

Graph-based ML

Deliverables:


Trade vulnerability indicators

Network-based risk visualisations

Ranking of high-risk trade relationships

title: List of datathon challenges
section: 18 Disease Risk Along Trade and Movement Networks
text:
18 Disease Risk Along Trade and Movement Networks
Problem:

Trade and movement routes can amplify disease spread, but are often treated crudely.
Objective:

Model disease risk propagation along trade-related networks.
Data (pilot):


Livestock/product trade matrices

Outbreak locations

Optional mobility proxies

Methods:


Network epidemiology

Spatio-network models

Simulation approaches

Deliverables:


Risk propagation metrics

Network-informed risk maps

Scenario-based simulations

title: List of datathon challenges
section: 19 Science-Based Regionalisation and Zoning Indicators
text:
19 Science-Based Regionalisation and Zoning Indicators
Problem:

Regionalisation decisions are critical but analytically under-supported.
Objective:

Design quantitative indicators supporting zoning/regionalisation under outbreaks.
Data (pilot):


Subnational outbreak data (or synthetic)

Trade exposure proxies

Control measures metadata

Methods:


Spatial risk aggregation

Scenario modelling

Uncertainty-aware indicators

Deliverables:


Zoning and regionalisation indicators

Scenario comparisons under uncertainty

Decision-support visual outputs

title: List of datathon challenges
section: 20 Consistency of Sanitary Measures with Observed Risk
text:
20 Consistency of Sanitary Measures with Observed Risk
Problem:

Trade restrictions may not always align with epidemiological risk.
Objective:

Compare observed trade measures with estimated disease risk.
Data (pilot):


Outbreak and risk estimates

Trade policy proxies (binary/ordinal)

Methods:


Risk–response analysis

Classification and clustering

Deliverables:


Risk–response consistency metrics

Typology of trade measures vs risk

Analytical framework for proportionality assessment

title: List of datathon challenges
section: 21 Reconstructing Sources and Pathways of Transboundary Animal Disease Spread
text:
21 Reconstructing Sources and Pathways of Transboundary Animal Disease Spread
Problem:

Despite extensive scientific knowledge on disease transmission, real-world pathways through which transboundary animal diseases spread between countries remain only partially understood. Current international standards often rely on experimental (in-vivo and in-vitro) transmission studies, with limited systematic use of historical outbreak evidence and notification data.
This challenge invites participants to analyse real-world evidence on outbreak sources and transmission pathways, using WAHIS immediate notifications as a primary data source—particularly the routinely collected information on the suspected source of the outbreak. Additional contextual information may be drawn from scientific literature, historical records, or expert-elicited knowledge (e.g. interviews, secondary datasets).
Objective:

Develop analytical approaches that:
Identify and characterise actual sources and pathways of transboundary disease spread.
Assess how these pathways vary by disease, species, commodity, region, and time.
Distinguish patterns driven by trade (official or unofficial), movements, or other risk pathways.
Key analytical questions

What do historical WAHIS immediate notifications reveal about the most frequent real-world sources of outbreaks?
How consistent are reported sources across regions, diseases, and commodities?
Are there discrepancies between observed outbreak pathways and those assumed in current standards?
Can outbreak source information be aggregated to generate decision-ready indicators for risk management?
Expected outputs

Aggregated analyses (temporal, regional, disease-specific, commodity-specific) of outbreak sources and pathways.
Methods to manage uncertainty and sensitivity (e.g. de-identification, retrospective analysis).
Visualisations or indicators that support evidence-based risk assessment and communication.
Insights that could inform WOAH Observatory thematic studies and future improvements to the Terrestrial and Aquatic Codes.
Impact

By grounding analysis in “learning from reality,” this challenge aims to strengthen the evidence base for trade-related risk management, support more targeted risk-based interventions, and improve how international standards reflect actual disease spread mechanisms. Findings could also highlight the need for enhanced collaboration with partners such as Customs and Border authorities when unofficial or illegal trade emerges as a significant driver.
